coredata.dc:title: Polylysine-modified near-infrared-emitting carbon dots assemblies: Amplification of tumor accumulation for enhanced tumor photothermal therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhenjian
Cheng, Hongwei
Wang, Bingzhe
Wang, Liming
Wu, Jun
Zhang, Bohan
Tang, Zikang
Qu, Songnan
coredata.dc:description: A key challenge to enhance the therapeutic outcome of photothermal therapy (PTT) is to improve the efficiency of passive targeted accumulation of photothermal agents at tumor sites. Carbon dots (CDs) are an ideal choice for application as photothermal agents because of their advantages such as adjustable fluorescence, high photothermal conversion efficiency, and excellent biocompatibility. Here, we synthesized polylysine-modified near-infrared (NIR)-emitting CDs assemblies (plys-CDs) through post-solvothermal reaction of NIR-emitting CDs with polylysine. The encapsulated structure of plys-CDs was confirmed by determining morphological, chemical, and luminescent properties. The particle size of CDs increased to approximately 40 ± 8 nm after polylysine modification and was within the size range appropriate for achieving superior enhanced permeability and retention effect. Plys-CDs maintained a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 54.9 %, coupled with increased tumor site accumulation, leading to a high efficacy in tumor PTT. Thus, plys-CDs have a great potential for application in photothermal ablation therapy of tumors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polylysine
Carbon dots
Near-infrared emitting
Tumor penetration
Photothermal therapy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR (Nos. 0128/2020/A3 , 0131/2020/A3 , 0007/2021/AKP , 006/2022/ALC , and 0139/2022/A3 ), the University of Macau (No. MYRG2020-00164-IAPME ), the Research and Development Grant for Chair Professor Fund from the University of Macau (No. CPG2020-00026-IAPME ), and the Shenzhen-Hong Kong-Macao Science and Technology Innovation Project (Category C) (Nos. SGDX20210823103803021 and EF029/IAPME-QSN/2022/SZSTIC ).

This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR (Nos. 0128/2020/A3, 0131/2020/A3, 0007/2021/AKP, 006/2022/ALC, and 0139/2022/A3), the University of Macau (No. MYRG2020-00164-IAPME), the Research and Development Grant for Chair Professor Fund from the University of Macau (No. CPG2020-00026-IAPME), the Shenzhen-Hong Kong-Macao Science and Technology Innovation Project (Category C) (Nos. SGDX20210823103803021 and EF029/IAPME-QSN/2022/SZSTIC), and the University of Macau-Huafa Group Joint Laboratory (HF-001-2021). All animal experiments were approved by the University of Macau Animal Ethics Committee under protocol no. UMARE-048-2023.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological characterization of CDs and plys-CD. TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of (A) CDs and (B) plys-CDs. (C) Diameter distribution of CDs and plys-CDs based on TEM images. AFM images of (D) CDs and (E) plys-CDs. (F) Height distribution of CDs and plys-CDs based on AFM images.

Physicochemical structure characterization of plys-CDs. (A) XRD patterns and (B) zeta potentials of plys-dots, CDs, and plys-CDs in aqueous solutions. (C) Full XPS spectra and high-resolution (D) C 1s, (E) N 1s, and (F) O 1s XPS spectra of CDs and plys-CDs.

Incorporation of polylysine enhanced the NIR-emitting characteristics of plys-CDs. (A) Absorption spectra and emission spectra (excitation at 655 nm) of CDs, plys-dots, and plys-CDs in dilute aqueous solutions. (B) Excitation-emission maps of CDs, (C) plys-dots, and (D) plys-CDs in dilute aqueous solutions.
Femtosecond transient absorption spectra of (A) CDs and (C) plys-CDs in dilute aqueous solutions. Time absorption profiles of (B) CDs and (D) plys-CDs in dilute aqueous solutions at 590 nm.

Biocompatibility and biodistribution characteristics of plys-CDs. (A) 4T1, HeLa, and H9C2 cells were incubated with aqueous plys-CDs solutions at various concentrations, and cell viability was assessed by the CCK-8 assay. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5 per group). (B) Mice were injected with plys-CDs for the indicated time points, and the major organs were excised to acquire NIR fluorescence images.

Photothermal performance of plys-CDs. (A) Photothermal effect of CDs and plys-CDs after irradiation with a 655 nm laser beam. (B) The photothermal stability of the aqueous solutions of CDs and plys-CDs was measured with three cycles of laser irradiation. (C) Cellular uptake of CDs and plys-CDs was determined using fluorescent images of 4T1 cells at 543 nm emission wavelength. (D) Live/death cell imaging assay of 4T1 cells treated with plys-CDs at 200 μg/mL concentration before and after laser irradiation at 655 nm.

Results of in vivo tumor penetration and PTT capability. (A) 4T1-bearing tumor mice were intravenously injected with CDs and plys-CDs at 100 µg concentration and then subjected to fluorescence imaging at various post-injection time points. (B) Infrared thermal images of mice intravenously injected with PBS, CDs, and plys-CDs and subjected to laser irradiation at 655 nm for the indicated time periods.

Evaluation of in vivo tumor inhibition by CDs and plys-CDs following laser irradiation. (A) Representative brightfield photos of mice after 12 and 18 days of treatment. (B) Tumor volume was calculated at the indicated time points. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5 per group). **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. (C) Representative excised tumor tissues. (D) The plys-CDs group was monitored for tumor recurrence up to 50 and 90 days after treatment.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of plys-CDs.

head.ce:title: Polylysine-modified near-infrared-emitting carbon dots assemblies: Amplification of tumor accumulation for enhanced tumor photothermal therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education, Institute of Applied Physics and Materials Engineering, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau 999078, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education
Institute of Applied Physics and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Joint Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education, Institute of Applied Physics and Materials Engineering, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau 999078, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polylysine-modified near-infrared (NIR)-emitting CDs assemblies (plys-CDs) were synthesized through post-solvothermal reaction of NIR-emitting CDs with polylysine. The obtained plys-CDs not only inherit the superior photothermal conversion efficiency of up to 54.9 %, but also exhibit significantly enhanced NIR fluorescence emission. Due to the increased particle size of about 40 ± 8 nm, plys-CDs exhibit enhanced the efficacy of tumor accumulation via intravenous injection, leading to improved performance in tumor photothermal therapy.

A key challenge to enhance the therapeutic outcome of photothermal therapy (PTT) is to improve the efficiency of passive targeted accumulation of photothermal agents at tumor sites. Carbon dots (CDs) are an ideal choice for application as photothermal agents because of their advantages such as adjustable fluorescence, high photothermal conversion efficiency, and excellent biocompatibility. Here, we synthesized polylysine-modified near-infrared (NIR)-emitting CDs assemblies (plys-CDs) through post-solvothermal reaction of NIR-emitting CDs with polylysine. The encapsulated structure of plys-CDs was confirmed by determining morphological, chemical, and luminescent properties. The particle size of CDs increased to approximately 40 ± 8 nm after polylysine modification and was within the size range appropriate for achieving superior enhanced permeability and retention effect. Plys-CDs maintained a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 54.9 %, coupled with increased tumor site accumulation, leading to a high efficacy in tumor PTT. Thus, plys-CDs have a great potential for application in photothermal ablation therapy of tumors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photothermal therapy (PTT) is emerging as a promising approach for cancer treatment because of its noninvasive nature, low cytotoxicity, absence of drug resistance, and minimal adverse effects [1–3]. The therapeutic efficacy of PTT mainly depends on the choice of photothermal agents [4–7]. Traditional photothermal agents such as metal nanomaterials have several limitations for their optimum use [8–10]. Metal nanomaterials are nonbiodegradable, and the presence of excessive residual metal elements following treatment can impair the normal functioning of the human body [11,12]. Additionally, their poor optical stability can lead to deformation of nanoparticles and reduced photothermal efficiency during continuous irradiation [13]. Therefore, the optimization of photothermal agents is a critical study area to further advance PTT.

Carbon dots (CDs) are known for their fluorescence monitoring capability, high photothermal conversion efficiency, good biological safety, and excellent stability and therefore have been extensively investigated as potential photothermal agents in tumor PTT [14–18]. The tunable bandgap of CDs allows for near-infrared (NIR) absorption and emission, thereby enabling deep tissue penetration. A crucial factor in achieving accurate and efficient PTT with CDs is their accumulation at the tumor site. A high accumulation level of CDs at the tumor site enables targeted tumor delivery with limited absorption or metabolism by normal organs. Moreover, enhancing tumor accumulation is significant for reducing the dose of CDs and minimizing potential harm to patients. The tumor accumulation of CDs primarily relies on the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect [19–21]. Tumors often exhibit irregular neovascularization, which is characterized by the presence of immature vascular endothelial tubes with window-like structures. Normal blood vessels typically possess an average pore cutoff size of 10–20 nm in diameter [22]. To ensure limited uptake of photothermal agents by the reticuloendothelial system and their selective absorption in tumors rather than in healthy tissues, these agents should preferably possess particle sizes within the range of 40–100 nm [23–26]. The enhancement of the EPR effect for CDs remains a challenge, which involves maximizing their effective accumulation at tumor sites and simultaneously avoiding decline in their photothermal conversion efficiency.

Previously, our group developed NIR-emitting CDs from citric acid and urea by using the solvothermal method with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO); these CDs demonstrated high efficiency in PTT [27]. Because of their small particle size, CDs undergo rapid metabolism in blood circulation, resulting in their short retention time in the body and limited accumulation at the tumor site. Hence, in the present study, we used polylysine-modified NIR-emitting CDs assemblies (plys-CDs) to improve tumor accumulation for PTT. plys-CDs showed a particle size of approximately 40 ± 8 nm. This increase in particle size significantly enhanced the efficacy of the EPR effect. Additionally, the modification of CDs with polylysine inhibited their interaction with water molecules. This strategy prevented fluorescence quenching in water and enhanced the NIR fluorescence emission. Moreover, plys-CDs exhibited a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 54.9 %. Furthermore, because of efficient tumor accumulation, plys-CDs exhibited considerably better photothermal performance than CDs, resulting in more effective tumor ablation.

DMSO (99.8 % purity), citric acid (99 % purity), urea (99 % purity), and polylysine (95 % purity, polymerization state: n = 25–35) were purchased from Aladdin (China). Deionized water was prepared using the Labconco WaterPro purification system. All other reagents were analytical grade and did not require further purification. Morphological studies were conducted using a transmission electron microscope (FEI Tecnai G2 F30) at 200 kV. An aqueous solution of plys-CDs was deposited onto a silicon wafer, dried, and subjected to atomic force microscopy (AFM) with the Bruker Icon system. An X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku SmartLab, equipped with a 9-kW rotating anode X-ray source) was used to conduct X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Zeta potential was measured using a Malvern Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments). By using Mo as the excitation (Ex) source, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). A Jasco V-770 spectrophotometer was used to acquire UV–vis absorption spectra, and an Edinburgh FS5 spectrophotometer was used to obtain photoluminescence (PL) spectra of all nanomaterials. Femtosecond transient absorption (TA) was measured with an Ultrafast Systems Helios spectrometer using Coherent Astrella-1K-F ultrafast titanium: sapphire amplifier (<100 fs, 1 kHz). An Al2O3 plate was used to produce broadband probe pulses (450–775 nm), with 400 nm pump pulses generated using a TOPAS-C light conversion optical parametric amplifier. Photothermal images were acquired using a FLIR E50 thermal imaging camera (FLIR Systems Inc.), and the laser (655 nm) was generated using a CNI laser HD-655NM-HS-3W-18061393.

The photothermal efficiency of CDs and plys-CDs was measured with UNT-T323 digital thermometers equipped with a K-type thermocouple. An aqueous solution of CDs and plys-CDs was added to quartz cuvettes and irradiated with a laser beam at 655 nm with a power density of 1 W/cm2 for 10 min. Pure water was used as a negative control.

The biocompatibility of plys-CDs with 4T1, HeLa, and H9C2 cells was evaluated by the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. Briefly, the cells were cultured in 96-well plates at the density of 5000 cells per well. After overnight culture, the cells were treated with plys-CDs at concentrations up to 100 μg/mL. Following 24 h incubation under dark conditions, the CCK-8 reagent was added at 10 % concentration, and the cells were further cultured for 2 h. The absorbance value was measured at 450 nm by using a microplate reader (Thermo Scientific), and the relative cell viability was calculated. The apoptotic effect of plys-CDs on 4T1 cells was determined by live/dead staining assay (Calcein-AM/PI). Briefly, 4T1 cells were treated with plys-CDs at 200 μg/mL concentration and then subjected to either 655 nm laser irradiation or no irradiation. The cells were observed with a fluorescent microscope (Olympus IX73 inverted microscope) equipped with a 20 × objective lens at 470 nm excitation, with a 500 nm long-pass filter.

In vivo biodistribution imaging of CDs and plys-CDs was performed in 6- to 8-week-old Balb/c female mice. The 4T1 tumor model was established by subcutaneously injecting 4T1 cells in mice at the density of 5 × 105 cells. Once the tumor size reached around 100 mm3, mice were randomly divided into five groups to receive phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), CDs, plys-CDs, and a combination of plys-CDs with phototherapy using 655 nm laser. Mice were anesthetized with tribromoethanol, and aqueous solutions of 100 μL CDs and plys-CDs at 1 mg/mL concentration were injected intravenously through the tail vein in mice. Following the completion of treatment, mice were sacrificed by cervical dislocation, and their major organs were removed for imaging at various time points (0, 0.5, 2, 4, 8, 24, and 48 h) following intravenous injection. An Andor iXon Life 888 EMCCD camera coupled with a 710-nm long-pass optical filter was used to acquire images at 655 nm Ex wavelength. The in vivo photothermal efficiency was determined using thermal imaging cameras (FLIR Systems Inc.). The tumor volume was recorded every two days and calculated as follows: V = ((tumor length) × (tumor width)2)/2.

Statistical analysis was performed using OriginPro 8.0 software. Data are expressed as mean ± SD. Multiple group comparison was analyzed by one-way analysis of variance. Differences with p < 0.05, p < 0.01, and p < 0.001 were considered statistically significant.

Precursor CDs were synthesized from citric acid and urea by the solvothermal reaction in DMSO, as described in our previous study [27]. A mixture containing 2 g of citric acid and 6 g of urea was dissolved in 30 mL of DMSO and subjected to solvothermal heating at 160 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, plys-CDs were prepared by dissolving 2 g of polylysine in the abovementioned mixture and subjecting the mixture to post-solvothermal reaction at 160 °C for 4 h (Scheme 1 ). To remove partial residual byproducts, the obtained dark solution was collected, mixed with twice its volume of ethanol, and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove DMSO, residual citric acid, and urea. The precipitate was collected and dissolved in deionized water. Subsequently, this suspension was dialyzed using 5000 Da dialysis bags (Spectrum Laboratories) against deionized water for 12 h to remove residual polylysine; the water was replaced every 2 h. The aqueous solution in the dialysis bag was collected and freeze-dried to obtain plys-CDs for further use.
Polylysine-derived nanoparticles (plys-dots) were prepared by the one-step solvothermal reaction of polylysine in DMSO at 160 °C for 4 h. The purification processes were the same as mentioned above.

The morphologies of CDs and plys-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In TEM images, CDs showed typical spherical shapes with sizes between 8 and 12 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CDs revealed well-resolved lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, which were located in the graphite (100) lattice plane (Fig. 1 A and 1C) [28–30]. After the solvothermal treatment of CDs with polylysine, plys-CDs exhibited spherical shapes with considerably larger diameters of 38–42 nm. Additionally, HRTEM images revealed that the particles of plys-CDs contained amorphous cross-linked polylysine-encapsulated CDs assemblies, thus demonstrating a hybridized structure of plys-dots and CDs (Fig. 1B and 1C). Consistently, AFM images confirmed an increase in the size of plys-CDs and the successful construction of plys-CDs assemblies (Fig. 1D–F). The increased size of plys-CDs was within the optimal size range for achieving an efficient EPR effect; therefore, we expected that the efficiency of tumor accumulation of plys-CDs would be enhanced.

The XRD patterns of CDs and plys-CDs are shown in Fig. 2 A. The diffraction peaks of plys-dots and CDs were observed at 21° and 26.8°, respectively. plys-CDs exhibited both diffraction peaks at 21° and 26.8°, thus revealing the hybridized structure of the combination of plys-dots and CDs. The zeta potential values are shown in Fig. 2B. plys-dots exhibited a positive potential at approximately 46.4 mV, whereas CDs displayed a negative potential at approximately −47.2 mV. Because of the hybridization of plys-dots on the surface of CDs, the zeta potential of plys-CDs was −32.8 mV. This alteration in the zeta potential of plys-CDs might be because of persistence of residual CDs on the surface of the hybrid structure. The hybridized structure was also detected in the Raman spectrum. The ratio of integrated intensities of D to G bands was increased for plys-CDs (Fig. S1). The chemical compositions of CDs and plys-CDs were further investigated by XPS. The full XPS spectra (Fig. 2C) showed that CDs and plys-CDs contained the same primary elements, i.e., C, N, and O, with indistinguishable percentages. High-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2D-F) confirmed the existence of CC/CC (284.6 eV), CN (285.4 eV), CO (286.9 eV), and CO (288.1 eV); pyridinic N (399.5 eV), pyrrolic N (400.2 eV), and graphitic N (401.2 eV); and CO (531.2 eV) and CO (532.4 eV), respectively [31,32]. Compared to CDs, after the solvothermal reaction with polylysine, the content of CN in plys-CDs had increased due to polylysine incorporation. The solvothermal reaction enhanced the crosslinking and interaction of polylysine with CDs, thereby resulting in increased pyridinic content and reduced CO content.

Next, we analyzed the optical properties of plys-dots, CDs, and plys-CDs. The main absorption band of plys-dots was located in the UV light range (Fig. 3 A). The hybridization of plys-dots and CDs enabled plys-CDs to exhibit enhanced absorption bands in both UV and NIR regions. At 655 nm Ex wavelength, the NIR fluorescence from plys-CDs aqueous solution was approximately 3.5-fold stronger than that from CDs aqueous solution of the same concentration. The Ex-emission (Em) spectra of CDs, plys-dots, and plys-CDs in aqueous solutions were acquired to evaluate their fluorescence imaging capability. CDs exhibited a strong green emission center at 550 nm and two weak emission centers at 625 and 700 nm (Fig. 3B). Compared to non-fluorescent polylysine, plys-dots exhibited blue fluorescence, which was caused by the crosslink-enhanced emission effect (Fig. 3C) [33]. Interestingly, the Ex-Em maps of plys-CDs showed both blue and green emission centers (Fig. 3D), thus indicating that plys-CDs inherited the luminescence properties of CDs and plys-dots. Moreover, the enhanced NIR emission of plys-CDs could be attributed to the hybridization of plys-dots. This modification partially reduced the direct contact of CDs with water, thereby diminishing fluorescence quenching through lower energy loss and surface protonation [34,35].

To further analyze the luminescence properties of plys-CDs and identify their structure, transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy was performed at 400 nm Ex wavelength. After photoexcitation, a broad photobleaching (PB) minima that correlated with the surface-related state was centered at approximately 560 nm for both CDs and plys-CDs, as shown in Fig. 4 A and C. Following their interaction with water and energy dissipation, CDs demonstrated a fast decay process, with a lifetime of 9.6 ps. After polylysine modification, plys-CDs exhibited an increase in the intensity of PB signals of the surface-related state in the first several picoseconds due to the energy transfer process from the core (kET = 7.7 × 1010 s−1). Additionally, because of the surface encapsulation of CDs by plys-dots, the lifetime of plys-CDs significantly increased to 869.9 ps. Thus, the post-solvothermal method coupled with modification using by a poly(amino acid) provides a simple and efficient strategy to improve the physicochemical properties of CDs.

A previous study showed that CDs have good biocompatibility [27]. Therefore, we assessed the cytotoxicity of plys-CDs in cancer cell lines (4T1 and HeLa) and normal cells (H9C2) (Fig. 5 A). At concentrations up to 1000 μg/mL, plys-CDs did not inhibit cell viability, thus showing low or no cytotoxicity. Based on the NIR emission properties of CDs and plys-CDs, their biodistributions were determined by NIR fluorescence imaging in vivo. Mice were intravenously administered 100 μL of aqueous CDs and plys-CDs solutions at 1 mg/mL concentration. After the mice were sacrificed, their major organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidneys, were surgically resected and subjected to fluorescence imaging at various time intervals (0.5, 2, 4, 8, 24, and 48 h) following intravenous injection (Fig. 5B and Fig. S2). The analysis of the NIR fluorescence signals in the main organs revealed that both CDs and plys-CDs were eliminated from the body of mice through the liver and kidney within 48 h after intravenous injection.

The photothermal effects of CDs and plys-CDs in aqueous solutions (200 μg/mL) were assessed using a 655 nm laser beam with a power intensity of 1 W/cm2. Both plys-CDs and CDs could rapidly activate and attain high temperatures (Fig. 6 A). The maximum temperature attained by plys-CDs was slightly lower than that attained by CDs at the same concentration. This is because, compared to pure CDs, plys-CDs have a lesser content of embedded CDs due to their hybridization with polylysine; this results in the lower absorption ratio in the red region for plys-CDs. Based on the absorption values of the aqueous solutions of CDs and plys-CDs at 200 μg/mL concentration (Fig. S3), the photothermal conversion efficiency (PTCE) of CDs and plys-CDs were calculated as 52.6 % and 54.9 %, respectively. The similar PTCEs of both CDs and plys-CDs indicate that the photothermal property originates from CDs, and polylysine modification does not impede the photothermal performance of plys-CDs. Furthermore, the aqueous solutions of CDs and plys-CDs did not show substantial deterioration in their photothermal performance even after three cycles of laser-induced heating and cooling (Fig. 6B); this finding indicated good photothermal stability of CDs and plys-CDs. Next, we examined the cellular uptake capability of CDs and plys-CDs by fluorescence imaging. 4T1 cells were treated with CDs and plys-CDs for 1 h. The cells incubated with plys-CDs showed significantly increased fluorescence intensity as compared to those incubated with CDs (Fig. 6C); this result indicated that plys-CDs could enhance the cellular uptake capability.

To determine the photothermal-induced tumor killing ability of plys-CDs, live/death cell imaging assay was conducted using 4T1 cells. The results showed that the cells exhibited green fluorescence prior to laser irradiation, thus indicating that the cells treated with plys-CDs were in living condition; this finding further confirmed the good biocompatibility of plys-CDs. Furthermore, the cells showed red fluorescence after laser irradiation treatment, thus confirming that the tumor cells were dead (Fig. 6D). These findings confirmed that plys-CDs have a potent tumor killing capability through the photothermal mechanism. Additionally, plys-CDs can achieve efficient tumor uptake for localized PTT therapy, thereby enabling potent tumor eradication while simultaneously minimizing toxicity to healthy tissues.

To confirm the tumor penetration ability of CDs and plys-CDs, we determined the fluorescence intensity of the tumor tissues after the intravenous administration of CDs and plys-CDs. The results showed effective accumulation of both CDs and plys-CDs in the tumor tissues after 0.5 h. However, after 2 h of intravenous administration, the penetration of CDs into the tumor drastically decreased, whereas plys-CDs were mostly retained in the tumor, as confirmed by changes in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 7 A). This result was consistent with the findings of the in vitro cellular uptake experiment (Fig. 6C). Thus, plys-CDs demonstrated an improved tumor accumulation ability, which increased the efficacy of the subsequent PTT session. The enhanced accumulation of plys-CDs within tumors validates our hypothesis and shows that the use of the post-solvothermal method with polylysine modification can enable to easily control the particle size to the ideal range required for the EPR effect. Next, we examined the PTT performance of plys-CDs in vivo after laser irradiation at 655 nm. Following laser irradiation for 300 s, the temperature of the tumor region injected with plys-CDs increased to approximately 65 °C. For the same treatment time, the temperature of the tumor sections injected with CDs and PBS reached approximately 47 °C and 36 °C, respectively (Fig. 7B). This finding revealed that the PTT capability of plys-CDs was greater than that of CDs; thus, PTT with plys-CDs can provide a more complete tumor ablation.

The PTT efficacy of plys-CDs was further evaluated by monitoring tumor growth. In the control group, tumor tissues exhibited continuous growth over time, and neither plys-CDs nor laser irradiation alone effectively inhibited tumor progression. In the CDs + PTT group, tumor growth was only marginally inhibited, primarily due to the relatively low photothermal temperature achieved (Fig. 8 A-C). In contrast, mice in the plys-CDs + PTT group showed a significant inhibition of tumor growth, and complete tumor ablation was noted with no tumor recurrence even after 90 days (Fig. 8D). These results indicate that plys-CDs exhibit superior in vivo PTT performance.

In summary, we synthesized plys-CDs through the post solvothermal reaction of NIR-emitting CDs with polylysine. The obtained plys-CDs not only showed superior photothermal conversion efficiency of 54.9 % but also exhibited significantly enhanced NIR fluorescence emission. Moreover, based on the increased particle size of approximately 40 ± 8 nm, plys-CDs exhibited enhanced tumor accumulation ability via intravenous injection in mice, resulting in improved tumor PTT efficacy than original CDs. We believe that plys-CDs could serve as an efficient photothermal agent in tumor PTT and this polylysine modification strategy may be suitable to other CDs systems to promote their clinical trials.

Zhenjian Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hongwei Cheng: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Bingzhe Wang: Methodology. Liming Wang: Methodology. Jun Wu: Methodology. Bohan Zhang: Methodology. Zikang Tang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Songnan Qu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

SGDX20210823103803021 and EF029/IAPME-QSN/2022/SZSTIC), and the University of Macau-Huafa Group Joint Laboratory (HF-001-2021).

ce:section.ce:para: All animal experiments were approved by the University of Macau Animal Ethics Committee under protocol no. UMARE-048-2023.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.04.153.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zitvogel, L. Apetoh, F. Ghiringhelli, G. Kroemer, Immunological aspects of cancer chemotherapy, Nature reviews immunology 8(1) (2008) 59-73.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Opportunities and challenges of radiotherapy for treating cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Schaue, W.H. McBride, Opportunities and challenges of radiotherapy for treating cancer, Nature reviews Clinical oncology 12(9) (2015) 527-540.
S. Liu, X. Pan, H. Liu, Two‐dimensional nanomaterials for photothermal therapy, Angewandte Chemie 132(15) (2020) 5943-5953.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterial applications in photothermal therapy for cancer
